    Chapter 5

    Chapter 5: Callbacks in ROS 2¶

    Introduction¶

    In standard software development, code usually executes sequentially from top to bottom. However, robotics systems operate in an asynchronous and unpredictable environment. You do not know exactly when a sensor will send data, when an emergency stop button will be pressed, or when a remote server will reply to a request. To handle this unpredictability without freezing the entire program, ROS 2 relies heavily on an event-driven architecture based on Callbacks.

    The standard function executes synchronously. You define the function, and you explicitly call it in your code. The program waits for the function to finish before moving to the next line.

    A callback function is a function that you define, but you never explicitly call it in your code. Instead, you pass the function's name (its reference) to the ROS 2 system. You are effectively telling ROS 2: "Keep this function ready. Whenever a specific event occurs, execute this code."

    There are few possible callback triggers such as:

    1. A message arriving on a Topic (Subscriber callback).
    2. A timer reaching its designated period (Timer callback).
    3. A Service request arriving from a client (Service callback).

    Until now we have alredy used this callback function in most of our code, for example in the battery_monitor.py with the battery_callback but also in the status client and server. We have also use a function, at the end of our codes, called rclpy.spin(node). This is a blocking function that takes complet control of the thread and runs an infinite loop of the file, this is necessary for example for the data incoming for a laser scan, ideally the code that must handle this data must run continuously in order to detect obstacles. Also our battery monitor must run with this infinite loops in order to detect when the battery drops.

    Of course robotics has many different applicationa nd also the possibility of just run once our callback could be usefull for checks the ROS 2 network for any pending events, and then immediately returns control to your script. This allows you to mix ROS 2 communication with your own custom infinite loops. This possibility is rclpy.spin_once(node, timeout_sec).

    Lets create a custom node for remote command check.

    Create a new file inside the amrj16_battery_managment pkg called remote_command.py and paste this code:

    In [ ]:
    Copied! 
    #!/usr/bin/env python3

import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from std_msgs.msg import String
import time

class RemoteCommand(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('remote_command_node')
        
        self.subscription = self.create_subscription(
            String,
            '/remote_command',
            self.command_callback,
            10)
            
        self.latest_command = "None"

    #define the callback function
    def command_callback(self, msg):
        self.get_logger().info(f'Callback Triggered! Received: {msg.data}')
        self.latest_command = msg.data

    # define a standard function
    def read_proprietary_hardware(self):
        # this simulate a sensor readings
        return "Hardware OK"

def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    node = RemoteCommand()
    
    node.get_logger().info('Starting remote command control loop')

    
    while rclpy.ok():
        # synchronous reading from the fake hardware function
        hw_status = node.read_proprietary_hardware()
        print(f"[Main Loop] Status: {hw_status} | Last Cmd: {node.latest_command}")
            
        #if a message arrives, it triggers the callback and moves on.
        rclpy.spin_once(node, timeout_sec=0.5)
            
    
    finally:
        node.destroy_node()
        rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
    #!/usr/bin/env python3 import rclpy from rclpy.node import Node from std_msgs.msg import String import time class RemoteCommand(Node): def __init__(self): super().__init__('remote_command_node') self.subscription = self.create_subscription( String, '/remote_command', self.command_callback, 10) self.latest_command = "None" #define the callback function def command_callback(self, msg): self.get_logger().info(f'Callback Triggered! Received: {msg.data}') self.latest_command = msg.data # define a standard function def read_proprietary_hardware(self): # this simulate a sensor readings return "Hardware OK" def main(args=None): rclpy.init(args=args) node = RemoteCommand() node.get_logger().info('Starting remote command control loop') while rclpy.ok(): # synchronous reading from the fake hardware function hw_status = node.read_proprietary_hardware() print(f"[Main Loop] Status: {hw_status} | Last Cmd: {node.latest_command}") #if a message arrives, it triggers the callback and moves on. rclpy.spin_once(node, timeout_sec=0.5) finally: node.destroy_node() rclpy.shutdown() if __name__ == '__main__': main()

    Update teh setup.py file with the new exe:

    In [ ]:
    Copied! 
    import os
from glob import glob
from setuptools import find_packages, setup

package_name = 'amrj16_battery_management'

setup(
    name=package_name,
    version='0.0.0',
    packages=find_packages(exclude=['test']),
    data_files=[
        ('share/ament_index/resource_index/packages',
            ['resource/' + package_name]),
        ('share/' + package_name, ['package.xml']),
        (os.path.join('share', package_name, 'launch'), glob('launch/*.launch.py')),
    ],
    install_requires=['setuptools'],
    zip_safe=True,
    maintainer='you',
    maintainer_email='',
    description='TODO: Package description',
    license='TODO: License declaration',
    extras_require={
        'test': [
            'pytest',
        ],
    },
    entry_points={
        'console_scripts': [
            'battery_status_exe = amrj16_battery_management.battery_status_node:main',
            'bms_pub_info_exe = amrj16_battery_management.bms_pub_info:main',
            'battery_monitor_exe = amrj16_battery_management.battery_monitor:main',
            'advanced_battery_pub_exe = amrj16_battery_management.advance_battery_pub:main',
            'charge_mode_server_exe = amrj16_battery_management.charge_mode_server:main',
            'charge_mode_async_client_exe = amrj16_battery_management.charge_mode_async_client:main', 
            'charge_mode_sync_client_exe = amrj16_battery_management.charge_mode_sync_client:main', 
            'remote_command_exe = amrj16_battery_management.remote_command:main',   #NEW LINE
        ],
    },
)
    import os from glob import glob from setuptools import find_packages, setup package_name = 'amrj16_battery_management' setup( name=package_name, version='0.0.0', packages=find_packages(exclude=['test']), data_files=[ ('share/ament_index/resource_index/packages', ['resource/' + package_name]), ('share/' + package_name, ['package.xml']), (os.path.join('share', package_name, 'launch'), glob('launch/*.launch.py')), ], install_requires=['setuptools'], zip_safe=True, maintainer='you', maintainer_email='', description='TODO: Package description', license='TODO: License declaration', extras_require={ 'test': [ 'pytest', ], }, entry_points={ 'console_scripts': [ 'battery_status_exe = amrj16_battery_management.battery_status_node:main', 'bms_pub_info_exe = amrj16_battery_management.bms_pub_info:main', 'battery_monitor_exe = amrj16_battery_management.battery_monitor:main', 'advanced_battery_pub_exe = amrj16_battery_management.advance_battery_pub:main', 'charge_mode_server_exe = amrj16_battery_management.charge_mode_server:main', 'charge_mode_async_client_exe = amrj16_battery_management.charge_mode_async_client:main', 'charge_mode_sync_client_exe = amrj16_battery_management.charge_mode_sync_client:main', 'remote_command_exe = amrj16_battery_management.remote_command:main', #NEW LINE ], }, )

    Test it with:

    In [ ]:
    Copied! 
    # compile
!colcon build --packages-select amrj16_battery_management

#source
source install/setup.bash

#run our node
!ros2 run amrj16_battery_management remote_command_exe

#publish a command 
!ros2 topic pub --once /remote_command std_msgs/msg/String "{data: 'ENGAGE_MOTORS'}"
    # compile !colcon build --packages-select amrj16_battery_management #source source install/setup.bash #run our node !ros2 run amrj16_battery_management remote_command_exe #publish a command !ros2 topic pub --once /remote_command std_msgs/msg/String "{data: 'ENGAGE_MOTORS'}"

    The resould should be this:

    The potential of this is clear, immagine to start a robot but you want to start the camera only when certian condition are met, this is the way to do it.
